dominant_error_pattern: Students confuse federal confidentiality statutes or overgeneralize IDEA to cover all student privacy, missing FERPA's specific applicability to this neighbor-sharing scenario.
instructional_red_flags: Distractor A (Federal Confidentiality Act of 1975) is a false law; B (no violation) assumes the specialist role justifies sharing without consent. Teach: FERPA applies when school staff shares records without consent, regardless of the recipient's qualification or relevance.

dominant_error_pattern: Students skip the parental consent prerequisite when discussing assessment results with a student, treating developmental appropriateness as a substitute for permission-seeking.
instructional_red_flags: Distractor A (hide results) and B (disclose directly) both bypass consent; D (all parties together) adds a procedural variation. Build the hierarchy: parent consent first → then discuss findings → use language matched to student age. Explain why parental buy-in matters before student interpretation.

dominant_error_pattern: Students allow parents to view or copy test protocols, conflating access-to-results with access-to-test-items, and violating test security and copyright protections.
instructional_red_flags: Distractors A (show and copy protocol) and B (show without copy) both expose the protocol. Teach the boundary: parents get scores, interpretations, and reasoning (B is correct)—but test items and protocols stay confidential. Use a sample report showing what parents receive.

dominant_error_pattern: Students assume lawyers' involvement triggers automatic, full-document compliance, missing the collaborative explanation pathway and misunderstanding FERPA's procedural safeguards.
instructional_red_flags: Distractor A (supply all copies immediately) over-complies; B (IEP review alone) under-serves the request. Teach: collaborate with parents and lawyer, review test data and interpretations, but withhold copying at this procedural stage. Reference the school's legal counsel if needed.

dominant_error_pattern: Students confuse IDEA and IDEIA with FERPA, conflating special education service rights with educational records access rights.
instructional_red_flags: Distractor A (IDEA) and D (IDEIA 2006) swap focus from general education records to special education services. Isolate: FERPA (1974) mandates parental access to all educational records for all students. IDEA addresses special education eligibility and services, a separate framework.

dominant_error_pattern: Students misidentify legitimate grounds for disclosure, confusing medical relevance, authority, or police request with FERPA's actual emergency exception criteria.
instructional_red_flags: Distractors A (medical doctor request), B (admin direction), and C (police order) are insufficient alone. Teach: FERPA's disclosure exception is triggered by genuine health/safety emergencies where the records are necessary. Requests from professionals, orders from police, or admin directives do not auto-permit disclosure without emergency cause.

dominant_error_pattern: Students misremember FERPA's statutory name or conflate it with IDEA or IDEIA, losing the correct legal reference needed for accurate professional communication.
instructional_red_flags: Distractors B (false 1974 law name) and A/D (IDEA/IDEIA) both reference incorrect statutes. Drill the accurate title: Family Educational Rights and Privacy Act (FERPA), enacted 1974. Create a reference card with FERPA, IDEA, IDEIA, and HIPAA titles and scopes side-by-side.
